properties.field_incident_overview:
This is the final update of the Type 3 team as they transfer command of the fire back to the local district as of tomorrow morning. 64 personnel remain assigned to the Billy Fire.

Unless there are significant changes in fire activity, this will be the last daily update of the Billy Fire.

-

The Billy Fire is a lightning-caused wildfire on the Tonto National Forest, 15 miles south of Young, Arizona. Firefighters are taking a full suppression strategy on the fire situated on the east side of the Sierra Ancha Mountains.
properties.title: Billy Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
Evacuations: Curry County Sheriff John Ward has removed all evacation levels for the Moon Complex. 

The Emergency Area and Trail closure for the Moon Complex that went into effect November 14th of 2025, has been rescinded. Effective, February 24th, 2026, this closure was replaced with Emergency Trail Closure 06-10-28-26-04, which re-opens many of the camping areas along the lower Rogue River Corridor. While much of the fire-impacted area has been reopened, recreationists will continue to see effects from the fire and ongoing repair work in the area. 

For closure information, visit: https://www.fs.usda.gov/r06/rogue-siskiyou/alerts/rogue-river-national-recreation-trail-emergency-closure 

The remaining Rogue River Trail Closure begins at the western trailhead of the Rogue River Trail (Trail #1160) and follows the trail to the east for approximately 8 miles to Jackson Creek. The trail from Jackson Creek to Clay Hill Creek will be opened by May 1, 2026. The Forest will be assessing the segment from Clay Hill Creek to Foster Bar in the spring of 2026. 

On September 3, a lightning event on the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest caused a number of fires in the vicinity of the Rogue River about 11 miles northeast of Agness, Oregon. Six of these made up the initial Moon Complex: the Backbone, Brushy, Pinnacle, Tate, Paradise, and Stair fires. 

The Moon Complex is now in 100% containment status. 

Fire Restrictions: Tuesday, October 16, 2025, The Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest decreased the Fire Danger Rating to LOW across all five Ranger Districts and removed Public Use Restrictions forest-wide as well as on the Wild & Scenic section of the Rogue River. To learn more, visit the forest website: https://www.fs.usda.gov/alerts/rogue-siskiyou/alerts-notices

Fire Repair: Repair of fire line and roadways is the primary focus of resources engaged on the Moon Complex
properties.title: Moon Complex

properties.field_incident_overview:
Road 203 Fire has been declared controlled. 

The Road 203 Fire was detected at 4:00 pm Thursday, March 12, 2026 on the Bessey Ranger District of the Nebraska National Forest. Ground crews initially responded and received assistance from air resources conducting retardant and water drops. Rocky Mountain Complex IMT 3 assumed command of the fire on Saturday, March 14, 2026.

The Road 203 Fire is burning in heavy dead and down timber and grass. The terrain is steep and remote, making it challenging for firefighters to get in or out of the incident. A Nebraska National Forest closure order is in effect.
properties.title: Road 203 Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
A large wildfire started in the southeast corner of Lincoln County during late afternoon on Thursday, March 12 and entered Dawson County later that night. Winds from the west were very high and quickly spread the fire.  Numerous evacuations took place as the fire rapidly spread to the south/southeast. Road closures occurred due to the impact of fire and smoke. Firefighters and community members heroically fought the fire since it started.  

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) assumed management of the Morrill and Cottonwood Fires on March 14.  At 6 p.m. MDT March 28, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 assumed command of the Ashby, Minor and Cottonwood Fires. Under the direction of the State of Nebraska, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 transferred management responsibility of the Ashby, Minor, and Cottonwood Fires to local volunteer fire departments on Sunday, April 5, at 6 a.m. local time. Local jurisdictions will also be responsible for the initial and ongoing suppression of any new fires that may start.

Resources will remain available to provide statewide initial attack response. As with its predecessor, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 will manage all fires under the direction of the State of Nebraska.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

Please visit this link for additional information sources.
properties.title: Cottonwood Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
A large wildfire started after midnight on Thursday, March 26 approximately 2 miles north of Ashby in Grant County. Due to high winds, it quickly ran 17 miles southward and burned into Morrill County and the area already burned by the Morrill Fire. An evacuation of Ashby took place as structures were threatened. Road closures occurred due to the impact of fire and smoke. 

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) mobilized initial-attack resources from the nearby Morrill and Cottonwood Fires. They actively engaged the fire and assisted local volunteer fire departments and the Nebraska State Fire Marshal’s Wildland Incident Response and Assistance Team. At Nebraska Governor Jim Pillen’s request, Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team (CIMT) 1 assisted with both the Ashby and nearby Minor Fire. 

The incident has additional initial-attack groups staged and ready to immediately respond to requests from local or state officials to assist with the initial suppression of any other new fires that may start. 

 At 6 p.m. MDT March 28, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 assumed command of the Ashby, Minor and Cottonwood Fires and provided statewide initial attack response.  Under the direction of the State of Nebraska, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 transferred management responsibility of the Ashby, Minor, and Cottonwood Fires to local volunteer fire departments on Sunday, April 5, at 6 a.m. local time. Local jurisdictions will also be responsible for the initial and ongoing suppression of any new fires that may start.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

Please visit this link for additional information sources.
properties.title: Ashby Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
A large wildfire started after midnight on Thursday, March 26 approximately 5 miles south of the community of Hyannis in Grant County. Due to high winds, the Minor Fire moved approximately 13 miles southward and nearly entered Arthur County. 

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) mobilized initial-attack resources from the nearby Morrill and Cottonwood Fires. They actively engaged the fire and assisted local volunteer fire departments and the Nebraska State Fire Marshal’s Wildland Incident Response and Assistance Team. At Nebraska Governor Jim Pillen’s request, Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team (CIMT) 1 assisted with both the Ashby and nearby Minor Fire. 

The incident has additional initial-attack groups staged and ready to immediately respond to requests from local or state officials to assist with the initial suppression of any other new fires that may start. The State Emergency Operations Center (SEOC) at the Nebraska Emergency Management Agency (NEMA) has been and remains fully operational to coordinate with Rocky Mountain CIMT 2.

 At 6 p.m. MDT March 28, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 assumed command of the Ashby, Minor and Cottonwood Fires and provided statewide initial attack response. Under the direction of the State of Nebraska, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 transferred management responsibility of the Ashby, Minor, and Cottonwood Fires to local volunteer fire departments on Sunday, April 5, at 6 a.m. local time.  Local jurisdictions will also be responsible for the initial and ongoing suppression of any new fires that may start.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

Please visit this link for additional information sources.
properties.title: Minor Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Sago Fire is a full suppression incident. It is currently burning in grass as well as ponderosa pine, piñon, and juniper trees. Crews are actively working to improve and keep the fire within established retardant lines, handlines, and dozer lines. Repair work began on Tuesday, March 31, 2026. Command of the Sago Fire on the Mescalero Reservation was transferred to the Gila Las Cruces Type 3 Incident Management Team Friday at 5 p.m.
properties.title: Sago Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The 79 Fire was declared 100% contained at 5,408 acres on Tuesday afternoon 4.14.26.  State and local resources will continue checking on the fire for several days.

==

The 79 Fire was declared 4.11.26, three miles north of Buffalo Gap, South Dakota. There are state and federal resources supporting the suppression effort.
properties.title: 79 Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The East Side Fire ignited on April 20, 2026. 

 All evacuation orders and warnings for the East Side Fire were lifted Wednesday, April 22.
properties.title: East Side Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Gun Range Fire is located on the Osceola National Forest, one mile northeast of Watertown, Florida. The current size is 129 acres with 85% containment. The fire was detected on April 19th. The cause is undetermined.

The wildfire remains within established control lines with minimal fire behavior expected. Firefighters continue to focus on hot spot detection, removing hazard trees and structure protection. 

The Lewis D. Whitaker Osceola Shooting Range is closed until further notice per the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission. 

For current smoke information, visit https://airnow.org

Closures and Restrictions: 

  • Columbia and Baker Counties are under a temporary burn ban. 
  • Open burning of yard debris is prohibited year-round per county ordinance. 

Regarding drones: Please remember, if you fly we can’t!
properties.title: Gun Range

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Sand Drain Fire is located 18 miles northeast of Olustee, Florida on the Osceola National Forest. This human-caused wildfire started April 12th. The size remains at 56 acres and is 100 percent contained as of May 6th, 2026. The fire is currently being managed by U.S. Forest Service - National Forests in Florida personnel.

The Fire remains within established containment lines. Firefighters continue to focus on hot spot detection and hazard tree removal. Structure protection and initial attack resources remain in place. 

For current smoke information, visit airnow.org

Closures and Restrictions: 

  • Columbia and Baker Counties are under a temporary burn ban. 
  • Open burning of yard debris is prohibited year-round per county ordinance. 
  • The Lewis D. Whitaker Osceola Shooting Range is closed until further notice per the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission 

Regarding drones: Please remember, if you fly we can’t!
properties.title: Sand Drain

properties.field_incident_overview:
Reported: 4/22/26 at 6:36 pm

Location: Samuel R. McKelvie National Forest

Jurisdiction: Federal

Andrews Fire Afternoon Update 04-23-2026

The Andrews Fire has been declared 100% contained. Upon more accurating mapping it was determined that the fire is 570 acres. 

Crews will continue to patrol the fire's permieter and within it, doing mop-up and montioring over the next several days. 

The Red Flag Warning is still in effect until 8:00 PM CDT. 

Andrews Fire Morning Update 04-23-2026

The Andrews Fire was reported on April 22, 2026 at approximately 6:36 PM on the Samuel R. McKelvie National Forest. The current estimated burn area is 2500 acres and is being managed by Nebraska National Forests and Grasslands wildland fire crews. The fire has been burning in the north central area of the McKelvie National Forest in grass fuels.

Crews worked through the night with successful suppression efforts and are currently seeing minimal activity. Current strategy for today and the following days will be patrolling, mop-up, and monitoring the burned and active fire areas. Firefighters doing mop-up is a critical part of wildland fire efforts, firefighters extinguish hot spots to prevent reignition.

There are multiple active new wildfires adjacent to the McKelvie National Forest that US Forest Service wildland fire crews, along with local, state, and federal agencies are assisting with in the area.

There is currently a Red Flag Warning in effect through 8:00 PM today, April 23, 2026. Strong winds are forecast to continue throughout today.

Stage 1 Fire Prohibitions remain in place for Nebraska National Forests and Grasslands units.
properties.title: Andrews Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The lightning caused Hummingbird Fire is burning in the Gila Wilderness through dense patches of brush, dead and down fuels, and standing dead trees (snags) left behind from Whitewater-Baldy fire in 2012. After an aggressive initial attack by ground resources, 2 large airtankers and a Type 1 helicopter, the fire continued to grow. The Gila Las Cruces Type 3 Team assumed command of the incident on April 23, 2026. Command was transferred to a type 5 organization on May 20th.
properties.title: Hummingbird Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Hazen Fire transitioned to a Type 4 Organization on Friday, May 8 at 7am. Future updates will be made to this incident as notable activity occurs.

 

History:

The Hazen Fire was detected on Saturday, May 2 at approximately 2:50 pm. The fire is burning in salt cedar, grass and brush approximately 1 mile southwest of Buckeye, AZ. The fire is in the response area for the City of Buckeye Fire Department and AFMA. They provided initial response along with other departments to include Goodyear Fire Department, Surprise Fire-Medical Department and others.  

Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management (DFFM) assumed command of the fire the evening of May 2 at the request of City of Buckeye Fire Department and Arizona Fire & Medical Authority (AFMA). The incident was under the command of a DFFM Type 3 Incident Management Team. Official updates will be provided on social media accounts for DFFM. Updates are provided as they occur and are verified for accuracy.
properties.title: Hazen

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Jones Fire is 100% contained at 84 acres. All resources have been released and the incident was turned back over to the Wickenburg Fire Department for any further monitoring. All objectives have been met, including fence repair and dozer line rehabilitation. There is no heat remaining along the fire's edge and no visible smokes showing. 

On Friday, crews finished all chainsaw operations and continued line improvement and mop up along the southwest side of the fire. Today's work also included repairing dozer line within the river bottom to clean it up and restore the area to its natural state. 

Starting today, Stage 1 fire restrictions are in place within State Trust lands in Gila, Maricopa, and Pinal Counties. For fire restriction information: WildlandFire.az.gov. 

The US 60 is fully open. Drivers should use caution through the area as engines continue to work alongside the road. 

 

The Jones Fire was reported on May 11 burning approximately 1 mile southeast of Wickenburg, AZ.  Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management (DFFM) is in command of the fire. The fire is burning in the Hassayampa River bottom in salt cedar, cottonwood, grass and brush.

Forward progression was stopped at 6:35pm on May 11.  The fire is under full suppression.

Evacuation Shelter: Red Cross Arizona has opened a shelter for evacuees at Wickenburg High School Gym located 1090 S. Vulture Mine Road, Wickenburg, AZ 85390

Traffic Information: visit az511.com
properties.title: Jones

properties.field_incident_overview:
Ash Pole Fire was discovered 5.13.2026, at 10:36pm.

5.26.2026 Update:

Today, 5/26/2026 Ash Pole Fire sits at 1028 acres and 100% contained.
properties.title: Ash Pole Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
At the end of the shift on June 11, the Seven Cabins Fire reached 100 percent containment, and all planned containment and repair operations were completed. While this marks a significant milestone in the response effort, it does not mean that all personnel have been released from the incident. Local fire resources will remain on scene and will continue to visit the area over the next few weeks. Restoration work will also continue in isolated areas, with the guidance of the Lincoln National Forest Resource Advisor. This work supports recovery of the affected landscape and helps reduce erosion post-fire.

The Seven Cabins Fire started on May 14, 2026, in the Capitan Mountains. The Pecos Zone Type 3 IMT took over management of the fire on June 10, 2026.
properties.title: Seven Cabins Fire

properties.field_incident_description: Wildfire across three counties in Southwest Kansas. The counties of Morton, Meade, and Clark.
properties.field_incident_overview:
On May 14, 2026 a powerful storm system moved through southwest Kansas. Lightning strikes, fueled by strong winds and very dry vegetation, started multiple fires across the landscape. The fires quickly surpassed the local and mutual aid response. State assistance was requested. Aviation, KFS DFMOs, and in-state Wildfire Taskforces were mobilized. Many of the initial fires merged and are now being managed as a complex fire. Kansas Governor Kelly issued a State of Emergency Declaration on Friday, May 15, 2026. Local declarations have been issued; there is no federal emergency declaration. 

On Monday, May 18, the Meade County Fires are now under one Incident Command System and will be called the Meade Lake Complex. 

On May 20, 2026, the fire region received precipitation and higher humidity, which helped increase containment on both complexes. Non-local resources will be returned to their home units, and the fires will be monitored and patrolled by the local fire departments. The weather conditions in southwest Kansas have not dramatically changed. There is still potential for new starts to burn intensely and grow rapidly. 

The fires under the Meade Lake Complex are the Wolf Canyon Fire, the Meade County Complex 1 and 2, the Proffitt Lake fire, and the 2 unstaffed fires in the southern part of the county.  

Meade Lake Complex -  Jeff Latham and Troy Mueller - Incident Commanders      

  • 91,616 acres
  • 100% contained

The 500-Acre Pond Fire and the Herman Range Fire grew together on Sunday May, 17, 2026. All fires in Clark County will also be a complex of fires. The complex will include the Bouziden Range Fire and the Lexington Fire

Herman Range Complex - Chad Maybery Incident Commander

On Monday, May 18, 2026, the Herman Ranch Fire made a significant run to the south when the forecasted cold front impacted the fire’s open containment lines along the southern edge of the incident. The acres and map do not reflect that event. 

  • 36,194 acres
  • 90% Contained
properties.title: Southwest Kansas Wildfire Outbreak

properties.field_incident_overview: The Beehive Fire ignited on June 8, 2026. The fire was started by a lightning storm in the area on the evening of June 7, 2026.
properties.title: Beehive Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The South Fork Fire ignited on June 9, 2026, in the Soldier Creek Wilderness area of the Nebraska National Forest’s Pine Ridge Ranger District near Crawford, Nebraska. Local responders, U.S. Forest Service crews and volunteer firefighting partners engaged the fire early as dry fuels, steep terrain and strong winds drove rapid spread. U.S. Highway 20 was closed by the Nebraska Department of Transportation as the fire jumped the highway. A red flag warning was in effect through Wednesday, June 10.

By June 10, the fire had grown to nearly 9,000 acres and Nebraska’s Governor Pillen officially issued an emergency declaration for the South Fork Fire. It was fueled by rugged terrain, dry vegetation including timber and grassy undergrowth, and strong winds. On June 11 at 6 am the Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 2, under the command of Jay Mickey, assumed control of the fire.

On June 12, containment increased to 7% even as the fire grew to about 28,067 acres. Resources including National Guard units, ground crews, dozers and aircraft focused on defending the towns of Crawford and the Fort Robinson State Park area. The Red Cross established community shelter options at the Chadron State College–Andrews Hall.

By June 15, containment was at 55% and the fire had grown to 38,914 acres. 581 personnel were supporting the fire. Forecasters predicted that the week ahead would bring sustained winds anticipated between 23 and 30 miles per hour, and the possibility of gusts escalating to as high as 50 miles per hour. 

Nebraska’s Governor, Jim Pillen received an operational visit of the South Fork Fire on June 16. He visited ICP and was provided with a comprehensive briefing. He had the opportunity to communicate with members of his constituency as well as incident team members. 

Containment reached 80% on June 17, with the acres climbing to 39,057. Although conditions continued to change, fire activity was generally quiet. A NIROPS flight (a thermal imaging aircraft used to map active wildfires) was ordered after several areas of hot spots were discovered. 

Line is now in place around the entire fire perimeter. Crews are working to secure and mop up the containment line and will continue for several days. Suppression repair work is also u begin underway and will continue in the next few days. 

On June 20th the overall acreage of the South Fork Fire increased to 39,696 due to more accurate satellite mapping showing previously unseen grassy areas and containment increased to 90%.

Rocky Mountain CIMT2 transfered command to the Nebraska Type 3 team on Sunday, June 21, 2026, at 6 p.m.  

Please follow the Nebraska National Forest on Facebook and the Nebraska National Forest website's Alerts and Notices page for further updates.
properties.title: South Fork Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: On June 9, 2026, aerial and ground firefighting resources began engaging in suppression actions on the Bear Fire, a wildfire located east of Mangus Mountain on the Quemado Ranger District of the Gila National Forest. The Gila Las Cruces Type 3 Incident Management Team, led by Incident Commander, Marcus Cornwell, assumed command of the Bear Fire on June 11, 2026.
properties.title: Bear Fire

properties.field_incident_description:
The Snake River Fire began on the evening of June 12th, 2026. The fire worked its way along Wawawai Road, approximately 4 miles west of Clarkston, in light flashy fuels and steep rocky terrain. Hand crews continued to gain access to the fire to cold trail, a method of controlling a partly extinguished fire edge by carefully inspecting and feeling with the hand for heat to detect any fire, digging out every live spot, and trenching any live edge, the perimeter of the fire throughout the day to increase containment. Crews worked on gridding 150 feet interior of the fireline around the entire perimeter of the fire extinguishing any heat they found. With the rugged terrain and unseasonably hot temperatures, crews had to navigate at a slower pace in order to safely work the fireline. Throughout the day state mobilization crews also assessed the structures threatened in the Moses community. With minimal fire activity throughout the day, two 20-person hand crews were reassigned to other nearby evolving incidents.

Northeast Washington Interagency Incident Management Team #1 has taken command of the fire as of 4:00PM on June 13th.

Evacuations and Closures: No evacuations are in effect at this time and there are no road closures

Weather: Temperature: High 91/Low 55. Mainly clear skies. Winds NE at 10 to 15 mph.
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Snake River Fire was reported at 5:55 PM on June 12th, along the Wawawai Road, approximately 4 miles west of Clarkston.  The fire quickly progressed north in flashy fuels (grasses and sagebrush) and in steep, rocky terrain, threatening rangeland, powerlines, communication infrastructure, rail infrastructure, and residences as Moses community.  Crews from Whitman County Fire Districts 14 and 12, Asotin County, DNR, and the nearby cities of Lewiston, Clarkson and Asotin engaged the fire, with the aid of a type 1 and type 2 helicopters.  State mobilization was authorized at 9:00PM on June 12th, coordinating additional district crews to respond to the fire. 

Northeast Washington Interagency Incident Management Team #1 has taken command of the fire as of 4:00PM on June 13th.
properties.title: Snake River

properties.field_incident_description:
The Rock Canyon Fire is a lightning-caused wildfire that was discovered on June 15, 2026.
Fire originated approximately 9 miles south of Highway 89 and 9 miles west of House Rock Valley Road in Coconino County, AZ
The fire's predominant movement has been eastward pushed by dry gusty winds from the west/northwest, largely parallel with the Winter Road.

Personnel are on scene from the U.S. Wildland Fire Service (USWFS), operating in coordination with the Bureau of Land Management, the Kaibab National Forest (USFS), and the Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management and local fire agencies are working to suppress this wildfire.

Firefighter and public safety remain the highest priority.
properties.field_incident_overview:
Rock Canyon Fire was discovered on June 15, 2026, at 1:47 PM. 

The Color Country Type 3 Incident Management Team (IMT) will be transitioning to a local Type 4 incident management organization June 25, 2026. 

Fire Area Closures for both the Bureau of Land Management - Arizona Strip District and the U.S. Forest Service - Kaibab National Forest will be lifted effective 0600 on June 25, 2026. 

There will be no further updates on the incident here or on social media unless unforeseen activity warrants.
properties.title: Rock Canyon Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Dellenbaugh Fire began the afternoon of Monday, June 15 in the Grand Canyon–Parashant National Monument south of Mount Dellenbaugh. The fire is now 100% contained.
properties.title: Dellenbaugh Fire

properties.field_incident_description:
This is a lightning caused fire with a full suppression strategy.
The fire is burning in primarily locust, with heavy dead and down fuels.
properties.field_incident_overview: The Rio Fire is a lightning caused fire located on the Espanola Ranger District of the Santa Fe National Forest in Mesa De La Gallina three miles northeast of Chicoma Mountain. It was reported on June 16, 2026.
properties.title: Rio Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Grapevine Fire is burning in timber in the Clover Mountain Wilderness Area, 10 miles southeast of Caliente, Nevada.
properties.title: Grapevine

properties.field_incident_overview: The Kane Springs Fire is burning in short grass, brush, and timber, 17 Miles Southwest of Caliente, NV.
properties.title: Kane Springs

properties.field_incident_overview: The Sycamore Fire was discovered Monday, June 15, 2026, north of Globe on the Globe Ranger District. Firefighters are utilizing both direct extinguishment and indirect confinement methods as part of a full-suppression strategy. Ground resources are being utilized where the probability of success is highest due to the remote location, steep rugged terrain and poor access.
properties.title: Sycamore

properties.field_incident_description: The Pocket Fire, located approximately 7 miles north of Sedona, Arizona was report at 501 pm on Friday, June 19, 2026.  The wildfire grew rapidly in brush, juniper and ponderosa pine, prompting the Coconino National Forest ordering the Northern Arizona Type 3 Incident Management Team.  Soon after, the fire made a run uphill towards Oak Creek Canyon and the recommendation to the Coconino County Sheriffs Department to order a "Go" evacuation status for Oak Creek Canyon.
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Pocket Fire, which started on June 19, 2026, approximately 7 miles north of Sedona, Arizona, continues to back and flank through numerous large, steep canyons north of Sedona and along the State Highway 89A corridor. Steep terrain and the extensive canyon system continue to present operational challenges, with rolling material creating a significant risk of fire spread below the main fire edge.

Current fire behavior is characterized by backing and flanking fire with short uphill runs occurring where fire aligns with slope reversals or where rolling material ignites receptive fuels. Critically dry fuels remain highly susceptible to ignition, allowing for continued fire spread under favorable conditions.
properties.title: Pocket Fire

properties.field_incident_description:
Minimal fire behavior. Isolated heat remained well interior with little to no threat to containment lines
or values at risk. Heavy fuels will continue to hold heat until they are completely consumed.
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Iron Fire is 1 mile northwest of Eureka in Juab County, Utah. The fire was reported late on the evening of Friday, June 19. The fire quickly grew in size and was estimated to be 1,000+ acres by the following morning, burning into Juab, Utah, and Toole counties. Great Basin Complex Incident Management Team 3 assumed command of the Iron Fire at 6:00AM on June 22, 2026. 

On Saturday, June 27, the same incident management team took over command of the Cherry Fire at 6:00AM and will be managing both the Iron and Cherry Fires.

Please rely on official sources for evacuation notices, road closures, shelter information, and re-entry decisions, including:

  • Utah Fire Info: Utah Fire Info page on Facebook and X
  • Juab County Sheriff’s Office Facebook page
  • Tooele County Sheriff's Office Facebook page
  • Eureka City alerts: Eureka City website
  • Tooele County: Emergency Management Alerts

Daily Fire Fact Sheets and maps can be found on the News tab of this Inciweb site.
properties.title: Iron

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Flat Fire is a lightning-caused wildfire located approximately 9 miles southwest of Heber, Arizona within Navajo Count. The fire is currently estimated at 134 acres and is 83% contained. The incident will transition on June 29th at 6:00am from the White Mountain Zone Type 3 team to a Type 4 organization.

During the current operational period, fire activity remained minimal, consisting primarily of smoldering and creeping within the interior. Firefighters will continue mop up operations and perimeter patrols to secure containment lines and keep the fire within the established perimeter. Fuels consist primarily of medium logging slash, brush, and tall grasses.

A Red Flag Warning remains in effect through Sunday evening, with dry breezy conditions and near-critical fire weather expected to continue into next week. Gusty winds may produce interior flare ups and localized spotting potential over the next 72 hours; however, no current threat to communities, infrastructure, or other values at risk has been identified.

There has been no reported damage to structures or infrastructure, no civilian impacts. Approximately 192 personnel remain assigned to the incident in support of suppression and containment efforts.

Operational priorities remained focused on patrolling and securing the fire perimeter, completing mop-up where needed, implementing suppression repair, and maintaining containment.
properties.title: Flat Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The lightning-caused fire is located west of Darwin Ranch, near upper Gros Ventre Falls in the Gros Ventre Wilderness on the Jackson Ranger District of the Bridger-Teton National Forest, and is burning in heavy timber in steep challenging terrain.

Fire managers are implementing direct extinguishment and indirect confinement strategies on the Kinky Creek Fire. A direct extinguishment strategy is when firefighters engage the active flame front directly to stop fire spread. An indirect confinement strategy is when firefighters work away from the active flame front to steer or contain the fire.

The public is asked to avoid Kinky Creek Road; Kinky Creek and Clear Creek trails; and Brewster, Lunch, and Chateau lakes so fire crews can work safely and efficiently. The forest has issued a closure order.

The fire danger rating is currently “high” for the Teton Interagency Dispatch area, which includes Bridger-Teton National Forest, Grand Teton National Park, and the National Elk Refuge.

For other news, events and information, visit the Bridger-Teton National Forest website and follow the Bridger-Teton on Facebook and X @BridgerTetonNF.
properties.title: Kinky Creek Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The McCauley Springs Fire was detected Wednesday morning June 24, 2026, on the Jemez Ranger District. The fire transferred command back to the Santa Fe National Forest, Jemez Ranger district on Sunday July 5, 2026.
properties.title: McCauley Springs Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Cottonwood Fire started on June 22, 2026 and experienced rapid growth fueled by extremely dry fuels and weather conditions conducive to fire spread. First responders worked to evacuate the fire area on June 23. Great Basin Complex Incident Management Team 5 assumed command of the Cottonwood Fire on June 24, 2026. 

The fire destroyed several structures and has impacted forest recreation sites. Structures that are still standing have structure protection groups assigned to them to prevent further loss. Firefighters are working to minimize acres burned by constructing direct containment lines along the fire perimeter, and air resources continue to support firefighters on the ground with fire suppression objectives. Weather conditions, dry fuels, steep terrain, and limited access continue to be major factors as crews work to increase containment.

Live Air Quality and Smoke information is available on this page if you scroll down below the Incident Info section.
properties.title: Cottonwood Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Shingle Creek Fire started June 21. Idaho Department of Lands (IDL), the Forest Service, and multiple partners responded. Northern Rockies Team 1 assumed command June 25 at 0600. Command of the fire was returned to the Nez Perce-Clearwater National Forest July 2nd at 0600.

The fire is currently in steep, rugged terrain, creating challenges for firefighters. As conditions become drier, fire activity may increase, but the work that firefighters have done so far has substantially reduced the risk of fire spreading. Continued patrol will be critical as the season progresses to ensure the fire stays within its footprint.
properties.title: Shingle Creek

properties.field_incident_description: The lightning-caused Mt. Tom Creek Fire is burning on a remote mountain slope on the west side of Olympic National Park. The fire is burning on a steep, densely forested slope approximately 5 miles west-northwest of Mount Olympus.
properties.field_incident_overview:
Situation: The lightning-caused Mt. Tom Creek Fire is burning high on a remote mountain slope on the west side of Olympic National Park. The area is in steep, densely forested terrain approximately five miles west‑northwest of Mount Olympus. Backpackers first reported the fire on June 24th, the morning after a lightning storm swept through the area.

The fire is approximately six miles from the Hoh Rain Forest administrative site. The Hoh Rain Forest Visitor Center, campground, and trails all remain open to the public.

Public and firefighter safety are the top priority as crews work to suppress this fire. Firefighter rappelers surveyed the area and determined that inserting crews directly from aircraft was not feasible. The fire is too far from potential landing zones and established trails that could serve as evacuation routes, making on‑the‑ground operations risky. Steep slopes, dense vegetation, and standing dead trees add further hazards for firefighters working in this rugged landscape.

Though high relative humidity and periodic precipitation may constrain fire behavior, they are unlikely to extinguish the fire.

Smoke: Smoke may be visible from various locations on the Olympic Peninsula. Air quality can change quickly.  For current smoke information, visit: https://fire.airnow.gov

Fire Restrictions: Olympic National Park implemented fire restrictions June 25th. Dispersed and backcountry fires are prohibited. Fires within established metal fire rings in developed campgrounds and picnic areas are exempt from this prohibition. Propane and gas stoves are allowed, if operated at least three feet away from flammable vegetation and forest litter.

Air Space Restrictions: A Temporary Flight Restriction is in place. If you fly, we can’t. Every time a drone is spotted near the fire all aircraft are grounded until we can be sure the drone is clear of this area: http://knowbeforeyoufly.org
properties.title: Mount Tom Creek Fire

properties.field_incident_description:
The Steamboat Fire was discovered on June 26, 2026 at approximately 1:15 p.m.
the fire was declared contained on July 1, 2026.
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Steamboat Fire was reported the afternoon of June 25, 2026 and declared contained on July 1, 2026.

 

AZ-GID Steamboat Fire Perimeter Map - 07-02-2026
Thu, 07/02/2026 - 11:47
AZ-GID Steamboat Fire Perimeter Map - 07-02-2026

Steamboat Fire Perimeter Map - July 2, 2026.
properties.title: Steamboat Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Beehive Fire was first reported at approximately 1 p.m. on June 26 in the Tusas Valley, north of Highway 64 and about 15 miles west of Tres Piedras, New Mexico. The fire was discovered by the crew of a detection flight that had been dispatched to survey the forest following recent lightning activity across the area.

As the fire grew and suppression needs increased, management of the incident was transitioned to Southwest Incident Management Team 3, led by Incident Commander Steven Griego. The team is coordinating firefighting resources and working closely with local, state, and federal partners to implement strategies that prioritize firefighter and public safety while protecting natural and cultural resources and minimizing impacts to nearby communities and infrastructure.

Important Information

  • All Updates
  • Maps
  • Current Fire Restrictions
  • Beehive Fire Closures
properties.title: Beehive Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Parsnip Peak Fire is burning in the remote Parsnip Peak Wilderness Area and only accessible by helicopter. Fuel moisture levels are at critical levels.
properties.title: Parsnip Peak

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Wild Goose Fire began on June 26, 2026 just south of Wild Goose Canyon on the Fishlake National Forest. It is located east of Holden and south of Scipio, Utah.

The fire is burning in continuous heavy dead and down fuels, steep terrain and standing timber 
fuel types. Fuels are exceptionally dry for this time of year and contributing to extreme fire behavior. Probability of ignition is near 80-100%. The fire grew very quickly in the first 24 hours and is continuing to spread.

Great Basin Complex Incident Management Team 7 assumed command of the Wild Goose Fire Sunday, June 28, 2026.
properties.title: Wild Goose Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Babylon Fire, which began on Friday, June 26, 2026, about 25 miles southwest of Monticello, Utah, has grown to 87,554 acres. This significant growth is driven by a combination of gusty winds, low humidity, and dry vegetation across the region. Firefighters are actively working to contain the blaze using a mix of direct suppression and indirect confinement strategies. There are currently 730 personnel assigned to the fire.

Due to active firefighting operations from the Babylon Fire, extensive public land closures are now in effect to ensure public and firefighter safety. All Forest Service lands, roads, and trails within the Monticello Ranger District of the Manti-La Sal National Forest are strictly closed, alongside the entire Needles District of Canyonlands National Park. Additionally, the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) has closed all managed lands west of Harts Draw Road, extending north through the Indian Creek Corridor to Indian Creek Falls, which encompasses Shay Mesa, Beef Basin, Dark Canyon, and Sweet Alice. For your safety and the safety of emergency crews, the public is advised to completely avoid the Monticello Ranger District and stay clear of all BLM and Forest Service lands south of Highway 211, west of Highway 191, and north of Highway 95.
properties.title: Babylon Fire

properties.field_incident_description:
The Cherry Fire is burning near Vernon in Tooele County, Utah. The fire has been active under hot, dry conditions and has affected the Vernon area. Firefighters are working to protect life and property, support local communities, conduct structure protection where needed, and construct and improve containment lines around the fire.

Current suppression actions include direct and indirect line construction, line preparation, and aviation support as conditions allow. Hot and dry weather may contribute to active fire activity.
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Cherry Fire was reported at approximately 10:30 a.m. Friday, June 26, 2026, and grew rapidly under extreme fire weather. It was reported six miles southwest of Highway 36. The fire is burning primarily in grass, sagebrush, and scattered juniper. It made a significant run Friday, June 26th, extending approximately 11 miles from south to north in only a few hours. 

Great Basin Complex Incident Management Team 3, which was overseeing the nearby Iron Fire, took over command of the Cherry Fire on Saturday, June 27 at 6:00 a.m. 

Please rely on official sources for evacuation notices, road closure, shelter information, and re-entry decisions, including:

  • Utah Fire Info: Utah Fire Info page on Facebook and X
  • Juab County Sheriff’s Office Facebook page
  • Tooele County Sheriff's Office Facebook page
  • Eureka City alerts: Eureka City website
  • Tooele County: Emergency Management Alerts
properties.title: Cherry Fire

properties.field_incident_description: The Far Draw, Doe Canyon and Ferris Fires began on June 27, 2026. The three fires merged together and will be referred to as the Ferris Fire. High winds are causing extreme fire behavior.
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Ferris Fire is burning approximately 15 miles northwest of Dolores, Colorado on the San Juan National Forest. Lightning was the cause. 

Strong sustained winds, low relative humidity, and extremely dry fuels are driving fire behavior. Crews are working to contain the fire as quickly and as safely possible but the volatile conditions dictate the actions that they are able to take. Fire severity varies, depending on the fuel type (trees, brush, grass), topography and weather.

Evacuations and Closures: Montezuma County implemented evacuations from the Bradfield Bridge Campground and surrounding areas along Highway 504 and Forest Service Road 521 to McPhee Dam. Dolores County has issued evacuations for private inholdings north of Glade Ranch and a pre-evacuation notification east of Lone Mesa State Park, east to the Dolores/Norwood Road, and north to just below Disappointment Road.  An interactive Evacuations Status Map for real-time information on evacuations. You can also follow Dolores County Office of Emergency Management for additional details. The San Juan National Forest has issued a Fire Area Closure.
properties.title: Ferris Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Willow Fire, located approximately 6 miles west of Leadville, CO, started on June 28, 2026. Southwest Area Team 4, lead by Incident Commander, Andrew Mandell, assumed command at 7:00 a.m. on June 30, 2026.

For current closure and evacuation information, please follow Lake County Office of Emergency Management. We ask that the public respect the closures and evacuation area and avoid the area of Mt Massive and Hagerman Pass.
properties.title: Willow Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Snyder Fire started on Saturday, June 26th during a Red Flag Warning with gusty winds, low relative humidity and dry fuel. Engines, crews and aircraft were used during initial suppression efforts.  Due to extreme fire behavior, crews were moved back to staging areas for their safety on Saturday night.  Crews returned to suppression efforts when conditions improved. The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 assumed command of the incident on Monday morning, June 29.

Links to official information for the Snyder Fire can be found at this website: Snyder Fire Linktree

An interactive map of the Snyder Fire is available on the National Interagency Fire Center GIS database. This map contains the fire perimeter for the Snyder Fire. You can enter a specific location and measure from that location to the edge of the fire. 

 

Pre-evacuations As of July 1, 2026, at 7 p.m. all pre-evacuation and evacuation notices issued in response to the Snyder Fire near Glade Park, Colorado have been lifted by the Mesa County Sheriff. Visit the Sheriff's Facebook page for current status of road closures.

 

Sign up for Emergency Alerts at the Grand Junction Regional Communication Center website

 

Colorado Parks & Wildlife Area Closures related to Snyder Fire can be found at this link: Colorado River closed to all public access and downstream recreation from Fruita to the Utah state line | Colorado Parks and Wildlife

 

Bureau of Land Management Closures related to the Snyder Fire can be found at these links:

BLM Revised Snyder Fire Area Closure

Upper Colorado River District Fire Restrictions

Grand Junction Field Office | Bureau of Land Management

 

Fire restrictions in Mesa County, the Grand Mesa, Uncompahgre and Gunnison National Forests and the BLM Grand Junction Field Office can be found at these links:

Fire restrictions from Emergency Services | Mesa County

Grand Mesa, Uncompahgre and Gunnison National Forests | GMUG National Forests Move to Stage 2 Fire Restrictions | Forest Service

Stage 2 Fire Restrictions | Bureau of Land Management

 

A Temporary Flight Restriction has been issued due to the use of firefighting aircraft. Details can be found at this link: Snyder Fire Temporary Flight Restriction. Wildfires are a no-fly zone for unauthorized aircraft, including drones. If you fly, we can’t. For more information, visit http://knowbeforeyoufly.org.

 

Governor Polis declared a State Of Emergency on June 27: Governor Polis Verbally Declares Disaster Emergency and Authorizes National Guard For Snyder Mesa Fire in Mesa County | Colorado Governor Jared Polis
properties.title: Snyder Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Gold Mountain Fire was discovered on June 27, 2026, at approximately 5:33 pm 2 miles northeast of Ouray, CO. Fire initially started burning in mixed conifer vegetation located on inaccessible terrain above the east side of Highway 550. Local, State, and Federal initial attack fire resources were ordered immediately. The fire spread quickly running up steep slopes. A local Type 3 Incident Management Team was assigned on June 27, 2026. Aviation resources were ordered throughout the next day, but high winds and terrain features shut down the use of aviation resources. Fire became very active, with group torching and crowning, increasing the fire growth to the east by 2,000 acres. Fire resources provided public safety and worked to protect structures. Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 3 was ordered on June 28, 2026, and took command on June 29, 2026, at 6 pm.
properties.title: Gold Mountain Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
Background: On the afternoon of Sunday, June 21, the Mogollon Baldy lookout detected a lightning-caused fire on Sacaton Mountain (elevation 10,658 feet) in the Gila Wilderness. The fire, which is on the Glenwood Ranger District, was initially about 4.5 miles west of the lookout tower. 

Firefighters quickly conducted an aerial assessment and estimated the fire—burning in brush, timber, logs, and snags—was about 2 acres and exhibiting moderate fire behavior. By late evening, the lookout reported the fire had grown to approximately 4 acres.

The fire’s location presents extreme risks to both ground and aerial firefighters: It is burning among numerous snags, a dense tangle of fallen trees, and thick brush regeneration from the 2012 Whitewater-Baldy Fire. The landscape is very rugged and steep (e.g., 40–60 percent slopes). Safety zones and escape routes are nonexistent. Emergency extraction times would be unacceptably long (i.e., 6+ hours) and logistically difficult. High-elevation flying, especially during hot weather, is very hazardous and an unacceptable transfer of risk to the pilot.

Smoke & Air Quality: Smoke is visible from numerous areas in and surrounding the Gila National Forest. Scroll down for air quality information and the AirNow Fire and Smoke Map.
properties.title: Sacaton Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Log Road Fire was first reported 11:37am June 29th.
properties.title: Log Road Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Aspen Acres Fire was first reported on June 29th at approximately 6:00am that originated 10 miles northwest of Rye, Colorado near the Aspen Acres Campground in the area of the San Isabel National Forest.

It experienced explosive growth due to record low fuel moisture levels and persistent red flag conditions in the week prior to and days following the ignition. The fire was wind driven by 40+ mph winds with gusts estimated to be up to 100 mph. It has impacted the community of Beulah, Colorado and the surrounding area in both Custer and Pueblo Counties.
properties.title: Aspen Acres Fire

properties.field_incident_description: Firefighters are on scene. The fire is currently smoldering and creeping in pine litter and brush.
properties.field_incident_overview: Mid-afternoon on July 2, 2026, the Long 2 Fire was detected during a routine fire patrol flight on the Gunflint Ranger District of the Superior National Forest within the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness (BWCAW). Firefighters were flown in by Beaver aircraft to begin direct suppression. The fire is smoldering and creeping in pine needle litter and brush. The fire has been determined to be lightning caused by recent thunderstorm activity earlier in the week.
properties.title: Long 2 Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: On 7/4/2026 @ 0029 a fire started and local resources began initial attack. Southeast Washington Type 3 Incident command took over command at 6:00 PM on 7/4/26.
properties.title: Chelan Hills
